questions.q: What is the photoelectric effect, and what is a photoelectron?
options: The heating of a metal by light; a photoelectron is a photon absorbed by the metal
The emission of electrons (photoelectrons) from a metal surface when light of sufficiently high frequency is incident on it
The bending of light around a metal edge; a photoelectron is the diffracted ray
The reflection of electrons from a metal; a photoelectron is a reflected light particle
questions.explanation: The photoelectric effect is the ejection of electrons from a metal when light of high enough frequency falls on it. A photoelectron is simply an electron liberated by this process — the name flags that light freed it.

questions.q: What does the stopping voltage \(V_0\) measure?
options: The total number of photoelectrons emitted per second
The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons, via \(K_{\max} = eV_0\)
The frequency of the incident light
The average speed of all the photoelectrons
questions.explanation: The stopping voltage is the reverse voltage that just reduces the photocurrent to zero by turning back even the fastest electron. So \(K_{\max} = eV_0\) — it measures the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons.

questions.q: The four photoelectric observations are summarised by 'DENF'. Which is correct?
options: Delay before emission; Energy from intensity; Number from frequency; Fixed wavelength
No Delay; Energy from frequency; Number from intensity; threshold Frequency
Doubled current; Equal energy; No threshold; Frequency irrelevant
Delay grows with brightness; Energy from frequency; Number from frequency; threshold Frequency
questions.explanation: DENF: no Delay (even for faint light), Energy of electrons from frequency, Number of electrons from intensity, and a threshold Frequency below which nothing is emitted.

questions.q: Photoelectrons have a maximum kinetic energy of \(2.6\times10^{-19}\,\mathrm{J}\). What stopping voltage reduces the photocurrent to zero? (\(e = 1.6\times10^{-19}\,\mathrm{C}\))
questions.explanation: \(V_0 = K_{\max}/e = 2.6\times10^{-19}/1.6\times10^{-19} = 1.6\,\mathrm{V}\). Joules divided by coulombs gives volts.

questions.q: At fixed frequency, the light intensity is doubled. What happens to the photocurrent and to the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons?
options: Both double
The photocurrent doubles; the maximum kinetic energy is unchanged
The maximum kinetic energy doubles; the photocurrent is unchanged
Both stay the same
questions.explanation: Intensity sets the number of electrons per second, so doubling it doubles the photocurrent. Maximum kinetic energy depends only on frequency, so with frequency fixed it is unchanged — this is the N and E of DENF.

questions.q: Why does the wave model fail to explain the absence of any time delay?
options: It predicts electrons are emitted faster than light arrives
It predicts that faint light should take measurable time to deposit enough energy in an electron, yet emission is instant even for very faint light
It predicts no electrons should ever be emitted
It predicts a delay only for bright light, but emission is instant
questions.explanation: In the wave picture, energy spreads thinly over the surface, so a single electron should slowly accumulate energy — predicting a delay that lengthens as light dims. Experiment shows instant emission even for faint light, contradicting the prediction.

questions.q: The existence of a threshold frequency is fatal to the wave model because the wave model predicts:
options: Light of any frequency should eject electrons if it is intense enough or shines long enough, so there should be no threshold
Only ultraviolet light can ever eject electrons
Electron energy should depend only on frequency
No electrons should be emitted at any frequency
questions.explanation: Since the wave model ties energy to intensity, any frequency should work given enough brightness or time — predicting no threshold. The observed hard frequency cut-off, untouchable by brightness, cannot be explained by a continuous wave.

questions.q: In which way is the wave model's prediction about electron energy described as 'backwards'?
options: It predicts energy rises with frequency; experiment shows it rises with intensity
It predicts energy rises with intensity and is independent of frequency; experiment shows energy rises with frequency and is independent of intensity
It predicts electron energy is always zero; experiment shows it is large
It predicts energy depends on the metal; experiment shows it depends on the light
questions.explanation: The wave model says brighter (bigger-amplitude) light gives each electron more energy, with frequency irrelevant. Experiment shows the exact reverse: energy depends on frequency and is independent of intensity.

questions.q: Which one of the four DENF observations is the wave model actually able to predict correctly?
options: No delay
Threshold frequency
Number of electrons from intensity
Energy from frequency
questions.explanation: Brighter light carries more energy, so the wave model can correctly predict that more intensity ejects more electrons per second (N). It gets the other three (D, E, F) wrong.

questions.q: What is the key exam technique for the question 'explain why the wave model cannot account for the photoelectric effect'?
options: Simply list the four observations
For each failure, pair the specific wave-model prediction with the contradicting observation (prediction X, observation Y, contradiction)
Describe the apparatus in full detail
Calculate the stopping voltage for each observation
questions.explanation: The marks live in the contrast: state what a wave should do, what is actually observed, and that they disagree — three matched pairs for the three failures. Listing the observations alone earns only about half marks.
